name: Kimberly Ranch
short_description: Private ranch site in southwestern Kansas offering chalcedony and agate nodules in semi-arid prairie terrain. Flat landscape with excellent visibility and easy ground-level collecting.
description:
Kimberly Ranch is a private rockhounding location in Kiowa County, Kansas, situated in the western High Plains at 2,168 feet elevation. The site features classic Kansas prairie terrain with flat to gently rolling grassland where chalcedony and agate specimens erode from weathered sediment layers and ancient creek beds. This remote rural area offers wide open spaces ideal for surface collecting, with excellent sight lines across flat terrain that make spotting specimens straightforward for rockhounds of all experience levels.

The Kimberly Ranch area sits within the Ogallala Formation geology, where Miocene-age sediments containing silica-rich nodules were deposited across the High Plains. Erosion and weathering of these ancient formations continue to expose colorful chalcedony, agate, and quartz material that collects on the surface after rain and seasonal weather cycles. The semi-arid climate and sparse vegetation allow for efficient ground scanning and recovery of quality specimens.

Collecting here involves walking the prairie surface looking for nodules and weathered specimens in exposed areas, stream beds, and eroded soil profiles. The flat terrain and open landscape make this a physically accessible site compared to rocky or mountainous locations, though the remote location and private land status require advance permission and careful planning. Successful rockhounding at Kimberly Ranch depends on securing landowner approval and understanding seasonal accessibility.
history: Kimberly Ranch sits within the High Plains province where Miocene-age Ogallala Formation sediments deposited silica-rich nodules and chalcedony-bearing gravels across Kansas. Weathering and erosion of these ancient deposits continue to expose collectible specimens on the prairie surface, making western Kansas a historically productive area for agate and chalcedony hobbyists since the early 20th century.
tips: Contact the ranch owner well in advance to secure permission and access - private land collection requires explicit authorization and respect for property boundaries. Search along exposed creek beds, eroded banks, and areas where recent rain has washed material across the surface; chalcedony nodules often appear as polished stones with waxy luster. Bring a good pair of binoculars to scan the flat terrain from distance before walking specific areas, and wear sturdy boots with ankle support as the prairie can have hidden holes and uneven ground disguised by grass. Early morning or late afternoon light creates better contrast for spotting specimens against dry prairie soil.
terrain_notes: Elevation 2,168 feet with flat to very gently rolling prairie terrain typical of the High Plains. Vegetation is sparse to moderate native grassland with scattered shrubs. Walking surface is compacted soil and grass with potential hidden holes and slight depressions. Access areas may be wet and muddy after rain. No formal parking facilities; park responsibly near ranch entry points. Walking distance from parking to productive areas varies based on landowner guidance, typically 0.5 to 2 miles across open prairie.
best_season: April - May, September - October. Spring and early fall offer moderate temperatures (60-75 degrees F) ideal for extended outdoor walking. Summer heat (90+ degrees F) can be intense on exposed prairie with minimal shade. Winter is possible but weather unpredictability and wet/frozen ground make fall and spring most productive and safe.
written_directions: From Kiowa, take US 54 west approximately 8 miles toward the Colorado border. Turn south onto a rural county road and proceed about 3-4 miles. Look for ranch entrance signs or contact local Kiowa County officials for specific private property access. GPS coordinates 37.586685, -99.182812 mark the general area. Verify exact access and parking with ranch owner before visiting, as private land access requires advance permission.
road_conditions: County roads may be unpaved dirt or gravel in variable condition. High-clearance vehicle recommended after heavy rain. Passenger cars suitable in dry conditions.
parking_notes: No developed parking area. Park in designated ranch areas near entry point, respecting property boundaries and gates. Capacity depends on landowner guidelines; typically space for 2-4 vehicles.
collection_rules: Private land collection. Must obtain explicit permission from Kimberly Ranch owner before collecting. No commercial collection permitted. Respect all property boundaries, gates, and access restrictions. Pack out all trash. Do not disturb ranch operations, livestock, or structures. Collection limited to surface material only - no excavation or disturbance of ground.
rules:
Obtain landowner permission in advance
No excavation or ground disturbance
Surface collection only
Pack out all trash
Respect property boundaries and livestock
No commercial collection
Follow all landowner access guidelines
nearest_services: Kiowa, Kansas (approximately 10 miles north) has basic services including gas stations and small dining options. Larger services available in Ashland (25 miles east) and Scott City (30 miles north). Bring water and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Flat prairie terrain is accessible to most hikers and families. No wheelchair access due to unimproved ground and lack of developed paths. Sturdy hiking boots recommended to avoid hidden holes and uneven grass-covered terrain. Generally suitable for moderate fitness levels due to flat topography, though remote location and distance from services require self-sufficiency.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Kimberly Ranch in Kansas?
faq.answer: Kimberly Ranch produces chalcedony, agate, quartz, and jasper specimens. The site's Ogallala Formation geology naturally concentrates these silica-rich minerals in weathered nodule form that erodes to the surface across the prairie. Most specimens are found as polished stones or partially weathered nodules that range from walnut to grapefruit size.

faq.question: Is Kimberly Ranch open to public rockhounding, and do I need permission?
faq.answer: Kimberly Ranch is private property and requires explicit landowner permission before collecting. Contact the ranch owner in advance to secure access. There is no public rockhounding allowed without permission. Always respect private property boundaries and follow all landowner guidelines.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Kimberly Ranch, Kansas?
faq.answer: April through May and September through October offer ideal conditions with moderate temperatures and increased specimen visibility after spring rains. Summer heat can exceed 90 degrees F with minimal shade on exposed prairie. Winter weather can make the site inaccessible due to mud and unpredictable conditions.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Kimberly Ranch for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Kimberly Ranch is excellent for beginners and families due to flat, easy terrain and straightforward surface collecting. The open prairie landscape provides clear visibility of specimens with minimal hiking difficulty. Children as young as 6 can participate, though adult supervision and plenty of water are essential in this remote location.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at Kimberly Ranch?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, binoculars for long-distance scanning, a collection bag or backpack, hand lens for specimen inspection, and sunscreen and hat for prairie exposure. A small hammer and chisel are unnecessary since surface collecting dominates. Most importantly, bring at least 2-3 liters of water per person, as the remote prairie has no shade or water sources.

faq.question: How do I find chalcedony and agate specimens at Kimberly Ranch?
faq.answer: Search exposed areas after rain when water washes material across the surface. Look along eroded banks, creek beds, and low areas where specimens concentrate. Chalcedony appears as polished, waxy-lustered stones ranging from translucent white to pink and brown. Use binoculars to scan the flat terrain from a distance before walking specific collecting areas to maximize efficiency.

meta_description: Find chalcedony, agate, and quartz at Kimberly Ranch, Kansas. Private site with easy prairie terrain. Permission required. Plan your collecting trip.
